Figma
01
Project Overview

A Recipe App
Built for How People Actually Cook

Rhymez Recipe is a personal project — a fully designed mobile recipe app that treats cooking as a social, visual, and personalised experience, not a reference tool. The insight behind the concept is simple: people don't open recipe apps the way they open cookbooks. They browse, they get inspired, they follow creators, they save things they'll probably never make. The design needed to serve all of those behaviours, not just the one where someone knows exactly what they want to cook.

The app spans the full user journey — from first open (onboarding and auth) through discovery (home feed and trending), to deep engagement (recipe detail with video, ingredients, prep steps, comments) and search (filtered results by dish name). Each screen was designed with a clear understanding of what the user wants to accomplish in that moment and what would make them want to stay.

02
The Design Brief

Cooking as
Content

The recipe app category is one of the most crowded on mobile. Most apps in the space fall into a predictable pattern: a search bar, a list of recipes with thumbnail images, and a detail page. Functional, but forgettable. Rhymez Recipe was designed around a different premise — what if the app behaved less like a recipe database and more like a cooking-focused social platform?

Recipes are followed — not just found. Chefs have subscriber counts. Videos autoplay. Users rate dishes and leave comments. The app borrows the social mechanics that make platforms like YouTube and TikTok compulsive, and applies them specifically to the context of cooking discovery.

🍜

Core design goal: Make the app feel as compelling to browse as a food video on social media — but with the depth and utility of a proper recipe reference when the user is ready to actually cook. The challenge was holding both of those experiences in a single coherent design.

Target Users

  • Casual home cooks — people who cook regularly but want inspiration, not just instructions
  • Recipe creators — amateur and semi-professional chefs who want a platform to share their work and build a following
  • Explorers — users who browse without intent, discovering new cuisines and dishes passively through trending and popular feeds
  • All three groups are served by the same interface — but the design ensures each gets what they came for within the first scroll

"The app should make you hungry before you've even decided what to cook."

03
Screen-by-Screen Breakdown

From First Open
to First Meal

Each screen in the Rhymez Recipe system was designed to serve a specific moment in the user's journey — and to make the transition to the next screen feel natural and desirable.

Onboarding & Home Feed

The onboarding flow begins with a full-bleed splash screen — the chef's hat logo over a richly coloured food photograph — that communicates the app's identity before a single word is read. The auth screen offers Google, Facebook, and email sign-up, plus a guest mode for frictionless entry.

  • Three federated auth options remove the password barrier for new users
  • "Enter as a guest" gives first-time users a no-commitment way to experience the app
  • Home feed opens with a personalised greeting ("Hello, Dave") — immediately making the experience feel individual
  • Trending Now horizontal scroll puts the most popular recipes front and centre above the fold
  • Popular Recipes section below uses a video card format with creator avatar, name, and star rating

Recipe Detail & Search Results

The recipe detail screen is where the app's social-platform DNA is most visible. It opens with a full-width video player at the top — the recipe is shown before it's described. Below the video: the dish name, creator profile with subscriber count and a subscribe button, full description, and then the structured recipe content.

  • Video-first layout mirrors the content format users are most familiar with from social media
  • Ingredients and Preparation sections use a clean typographic hierarchy — bold quantities, regular descriptions
  • Preparation steps use red checkmark markers — functional but on-brand, echoing the app's accent colour
  • Save Recipe and Share buttons in the bottom action bar make the utility actions always accessible
  • Search results display as a 2-column card grid — dish image, name, creator, and star rating per card
  • "Found 13 recipes" count gives users instant feedback on the relevance and size of results
04
Key Design Decisions

The UX Choices
That Define the App

Video Before Text

Placing the video player at the very top of the recipe detail page was a deliberate reversal of conventional recipe app design. Most apps lead with a hero image. Rhymez leads with motion — because a video of a sizzling prime rib roast does more persuasive work than any static photograph could.

Dark Crimson Brand Identity

The deep crimson (#8B1A1A) palette was chosen because it mirrors the colours of food itself — seared meats, rich sauces, roasted spices. The brand doesn't need to reference cooking explicitly because the colour palette is already food. It creates appetite before the imagery loads.

Social Mechanics in a Utility App

Subscriber counts, creator profiles, a floating "+" add button, and a Save Recipe action borrow patterns from social media and apply them to cooking. These mechanics increase session length and return visits — users come back to follow creators, not just to look up recipes.

Guest Mode as an Onboarding Strategy

Including "Enter as a guest" on the auth screen reduces the barrier to first experience significantly. Users who explore the app without commitment are far more likely to create an account later — after they've seen the value — than users who hit a forced registration wall and bounce.

Dual Discovery: Trending + Popular

The home feed offers two different content signals: Trending Now (what's hot right now — real-time, social) and Popular Recipes (consistently high-rated — evergreen, reliable). These two modes serve different user mindsets in the same scroll.

2-Column Search Grid

Search results display in a 2-column card grid rather than a list. This is a food-specific decision — food is inherently visual. A grid format shows roughly 2× the recipes per screen versus a list, increasing the likelihood that a user finds something they want to cook before losing patience and leaving.

05
Colour & Visual Language

A Palette That
Makes You Hungry

The visual system for Rhymez Recipe was built around one question: what colours make food look most appealing? The answer, consistently across food photography and restaurant design, involves deep warm reds, creamy whites, and accents that evoke heat and spice.

Deep Crimson
#8B1A1A

Brand primary. Nav bar, headers, action buttons. The colour of a perfectly seared crust.

Rose Red
#E8584A

Accents, links, step markers, section labels. Warmer and more energetic than the primary.

Lavender CTA
#7C6CF0

Subscribe button on recipe detail. Unexpected contrast that makes the social action stand out.

Warm White
#FEFAF9

Card backgrounds and recipe content areas. Warm white keeps food photography looking its best.

💜

The lavender subscribe button is the most deliberate unexpected colour choice in the app. On a screen dominated by crimson and warm photography, lavender creates an unmissable contrast that draws the eye directly to the social action — exactly what a creator-economy platform wants.

06
Takeaways & Reflections

What Designing
a Social-Utility App Teaches You

Rhymez Recipe sits at the intersection of two very different design disciplines — utility app design (clear hierarchy, efficient information retrieval) and social platform design (engagement, content surfacing, creator economics). Navigating that intersection required constant decision-making about which mode the user was in at any given screen, and designing specifically for that mode.

📱

Mobile-first changes information hierarchy

On a phone screen, you have roughly 6-8 lines of visible content. Every piece of information competes harder for position. This project sharpened instincts about what goes above the fold and what earns the scroll.

🎥

Video-first is a design decision, not a feature

Putting the video at the top of the recipe page isn't a feature request — it's a stance on how users engage with food content. Choosing what leads a screen communicates the product's philosophy.

🤝

Guest mode is a conversion strategy

Lowering the barrier to first use doesn't reduce account creation — it increases it. Users convert after experiencing value, not before. The "Enter as guest" option is a growth decision disguised as a UX one.

🎨

Unexpected colour creates emphasis

The lavender subscribe button on a crimson-dominated screen is a masterclass in using colour contrast intentionally. One unexpected colour creates one moment of visual priority — use it where it matters most.

🔍

Search results are a product, not a list

How you display search results is itself a design decision. Grid vs. list, card size, what metadata appears — these choices shape whether users find what they want or give up and leave.

⭐

Social signals reduce decision fatigue

Star ratings, subscriber counts, and "Trending Now" labels aren't decorative — they are the decision support system. When every recipe looks equally appealing, social proof tells users where to start.

"The best food apps don't just help you cook — they make you excited to cook before you've even left the sofa."

Rhymez Recipe
